Xbox Call-Attributed Profiler (XbPerf)

The Xbox Call-Attributed Profiler (XbPerf) allows you to analyze a title's CPU performance. The title must be compiled with specific switches so that it will produce profiling information. After running the title and collecting profiling information, you can transfer the profiling log to your development machine, where XbPerf can produce useful reports based on the information in the log file.

USAGE: xbperf [log-file [-run | -summary] [options] ] | [-collect [start [size] | stop] ]
OptionDescription
log-file File name of the log file that XbPerf should analyze. Remote files, specified with the xe:\... syntax, are copied to a temporary local location for processing. The local copies are deleted when XbPerf exits.
-run Specifies that XbPerf should operate in Run mode.
-summary Specifies that XbPerf should operate in Summary mode.
-collect start [size] Starts collection of profiling data, saving profiling information in the file specified by log-file. Without the optional size parameter, this option is equivalent to calling 
DmStartProfiling(log-file, 0)
from a title's actual code. The size parameter specifies the size of the memory buffer to use for collecting profiling data, expressed in megabytes. With the size parameter included, this option is equivalent to calling 
DmStartProfiling(log-file, size*1024*1024)
from a title's actual code.
-collect stop Stops collection of profiling data. If -collect stop is specified, then log-file must not be specified on the command line. This option is equivalent to calling 
DmStopProfiling()
from a title's actual code.
-maxdepth n Specifies that function pruning should occur after n levels. Functions deeper than n levels in the stack are collapsed to an entry of "..." in the output. This option is valid only in Run mode.
-threaded Separate call trees are shown for each thread. If this option is not specified, one call tree is shown, and thread switches are shown inline at the points at which they occur. This option is valid only in Run mode.
-sort column Sorts the specified column in decreasing order. Valid values for column are "callsin," "callsout," "incltime," or "excltime." If no sort field is specified, the output is sorted in decreasing order of exclusive time. This option is valid only in Summary mode.
-thread id Only functions called in the thread specified by id are shown. If id is not specified, each function entry includes all time in all threads. This option is valid only in Summary mode.
-pct Time values are shown as percentages of the total time attributed. In single-thread mode, this is a percentage of the total time spent in that thread. If -pct is not specified, all time values are shown in microseconds. This option is valid only in Summary mode.
-top n Only the top n function entries are displayed. This option is valid only in Summary mode.
-idle Time spent in the idle thread is attributed to the idle thread. If -idle is not specified, time spent in the idle thread is attributed to the last function to run prior to the idle thread (that is, the function that caused the wait).
-s path Tells XbPerf to use path as the path that contains the .pdb symbol file.

Preparing a Title for Profiling

In order to use XbPerf, you must first create an instrumented version of your game title. All object files to be profiled must be compiled with the /fastcap compiler option. Instrumented executables must also link with xbdm.lib to import required hooks and control functions.

Within the title's code, a call to DmStartProfiling begins collecting profiling data, and a call to DmStopProfiling ends data collection and flushes profiling data to a log file. Time spent performing data collection, including time spent flushing data to the log file, is not attributed to any function.

See the documentation for DmStartProfiling and DmStopProfiling for more details about how profiling data is actually written to the log file.

Profiling Without Adding Code

You may also generate profiling data without adding DmStartProfiling and DmStopProfiling calls to your title's code. The -collect start and -collect stop options allow you to start and stop logging while an instrumented title is running. Keep in mind that DmStartProfiling and DmStopProfiling allow you to restrict profiling to certain sections of your title's code, whereas the -collect options profile everything from the time you run XbPerf with the -collect start option until the time you run XbPerf with the -collect stop option.

Analyzing Profile Data

To analyze a log file with XbPerf, follow the steps below:

  1. Run an instrumented game title, and then exit it cleanly by using the Remote Reboot Tool (XbReboot) to terminate the title.
  2. Using the File Transfer Tool (XbCp), transfer the log file generated by the instrumented title to your development system.
  3. Run XbPerf with the appropriate options, specifying the log file as the first command line argument to XbPerf.

As an alternative to step 2, you may also use the xe:/... remote syntax to directly specify the log file's location on the Xbox Development Kit. If you plan to analyze the same log file more than once (for example, using XbPerf in both Run and Summary modes), it will save time to copy the log file to your development system manually. This is because running XbPerf on a remote log file copies the file to a temporary directory each time XbPerf is run, and deletes the file after XbPerf is finished.

The XbPerf tool has two different reporting modes: Run and Summary. When run in either mode, XbPerf writes its output to stdout.

Run Mode

Run mode produces a sequential list of every function call, showing the nesting hierarchy of the call stack from the point at which profiling begins until profiling ends. Because it mentions every single function call, Run mode can produce very large reports. Run mode is particularly useful for boot-up performance testing, because it shows where critical performance lags occur and offers clues for fixing them.

The following example shows XbPerf output in Run mode:

ThreadID   start time     duration   function
    0x14          284          322   thread-switch [IDLE]
    0x00          606          238   thread-switch 0x14
    0x14          844       453185   thread-switch [IDLE]
    0x00         2267         1444   thread-switch 0x1C
    0x1C         2390            2   main => ProcessKeySequences
    0x1C         2391            1   0  USBManager::IsButtonPressed
    0x1C         2393            1   main => CheckMUs
    0x1C         2394            1   0  XGetDeviceChanges
    0x1C         2396            4   main => D3DDevice_Clear
    0x1C         2400            1   main => D3DDevice_SetVertexShader
    0x1C         2401            0   main => D3DDevice_SetRenderState_ParameterCheck
    0x1C         2402            0   main => D3DDevice_SetRenderState_EdgeAntiAlias
    0x1C         2402          122   main => GouraudGroup::renderStrip
    0x1C         2403          121   0  GouraudObject::renderStrip
    0x1C         2403            2   0  1  D3DDevice_SetTransform
    0x1C         2406            2   0  1  D3DDevice_SetStreamSource
    0x1C         2409            1   0  1  D3DDevice_SetIndices
    0x1C         2411          113   0  1  D3DDevice_DrawIndexedVertices

The ThreadID column identifies the thread to which the function call belongs. The start time column indicates the number of microseconds since the start of profiling when the function on that line of the log started execution. The duration column represents the amount of time (in microseconds) spent in the function. The final column, function, identifies the function call and shows its hierarchy level in the call stack.

If your title is multithreaded, it may also be useful to run XbPerf in Run mode with the -thread option, which separates the report into separate sections by thread. The following example shows Run mode XbPerf output with the -thread option active:

Thread [IDLE] : time = 4102750
  start time     duration   function
         606          238   thread-switch 0x14
        2267         1444   thread-switch 0x1C
       31627         1125   thread-switch 0x1C
       65000         1029   thread-switch 0x1C
       98389         1312   thread-switch 0x1C
      131778         1105   thread-switch 0x1C
      165160         1099   thread-switch 0x1C
      198526         1012   thread-switch 0x1C
      231906         1120   thread-switch 0x1C
      265302         2590   thread-switch 0x1C

Thread 0x1C : time = 924194
  start time     duration   function
        2390            2   main => ProcessKeySequences
        2391            1   0  USBManager::IsButtonPressed
        2393            1   main => CheckMUs
        2394            1   0  XGetDeviceChanges
        2396            4   main => D3DDevice_Clear
        2400            1   main => D3DDevice_SetVertexShader
        2401            0   main => D3DDevice_SetRenderState_ParameterCheck
        2402            0   main => D3DDevice_SetRenderState_EdgeAntiAlias
        2402          122   main => GouraudGroup::renderStrip
        2403          121   0  GouraudObject::renderStrip
        2403            2   0  1  D3DDevice_SetTransform
        2406            2   0  1  D3DDevice_SetStreamSource
        2409            1   0  1  D3DDevice_SetIndices
        2411          113   0  1  D3DDevice_DrawIndexedVertices

The time value in the heading for each thread in -thread output specifies the total time (in microseconds) spent in that particular thread.

Summary Mode

Summary mode produces an aggregate report, showing the total time spent in specific functions. By default, Summary mode lists functions in decreasing order of time spent exclusively in each function. Summary mode is useful when you want to determine how much time is spent in certain sections of your title's code. For example, you could use Summary mode to determine if your game's AI code is wasting CPU time, or to test the efficiency of a specific search algorithm in relation to other code in your title.

The following example illustrates the output from XbPerf in Summary mode:

CallsIn CallsOut  InclTime  ExclTime Function Name
    200        0    338563    338563 XFONT_TextOut
    134        0    239099    239099 D3DDevice_Swap
   3216        0    104966    104966 D3DDevice_DrawIndexedVertices
     20   450660    143439     86351 AlphaTexture
    118        0     65903     65903 XFONT_SetTextHeight
  11212        0     59505     59505 FindNextFileA
 225280        0     45949     45949 D3DXCOLOR::operator unsigned long
    230        0     11541     11541 FindFirstFileA
 225300        0     10544     10544 D3DXCOLOR::D3DXCOLOR
    690        0      8837      8837 D3DDevice_DrawVertices
  10984        0      3533      3533 _strupr
    489        0      3491      3491 _snprintf
   3886        0      2299      2299 D3DDevice_SetTransform
    230        0      2252      2252 CloseHandle
    215        0      2155      2155 _snwprintf
  10984    11059      8837      1890 CXBEList::ProcessFile

The CallsIn column specifies the total number of calls made into that particular function. CallsOut specifies the total number of calls made by the function. InclTime represents inclusive time, or the total number of microseconds spent in the function and all of its child function calls. The ExclTime column lists exclusive time, or the total number of microseconds spent in a function, exclusive of its child function calls. Function Name is the name of the function call.

Instead of actual time values in microseconds, Summary mode can also produce percentages of total time for each function. Use the -pct option with Summary mode to generate percentage information. The InclTime and ExclTime columns specify percentages (rather than time in microseconds) when you include the -pct option on the xbperf command line. The following example shows Summary output from the same profile log as the previous example, but with the -pct option turned on:

CallsIn CallsOut  InclTime  ExclTime Function Name
    200        0     6.51%     6.51% XFONT_TextOut
    134        0     4.60%     4.60% D3DDevice_Swap
   3216        0     2.02%     2.02% D3DDevice_DrawIndexedVertices
     20   450660     2.76%     1.66% AlphaTexture
    118        0     1.27%     1.27% XFONT_SetTextHeight
  11212        0     1.14%     1.14% FindNextFileA
 225280        0     0.88%     0.88% D3DXCOLOR::operator unsigned long
    230        0     0.22%     0.22% FindFirstFileA
 225300        0     0.20%     0.20% D3DXCOLOR::D3DXCOLOR
    690        0     0.17%     0.17% D3DDevice_DrawVertices
  10984        0     0.07%     0.07% _strupr
    489        0     0.07%     0.07% _snprintf
   3886        0     0.04%     0.04% D3DDevice_SetTransform
    230        0     0.04%     0.04% CloseHandle
    215        0     0.04%     0.04% _snwprintf
  10984    11059     0.17%     0.04% CXBEList::ProcessFile

Remarks

Currently, XbPerf cannot profile DPCs (Deferred Procedure Calls).

For an easy way to view the profiling information produced by XbPerf, use the Xbox Call-Attributed Profile Viewer (XbPerfView).

See Also

Xbox Call-Attributed Profile Viewer (XbPerfView) | DmStartProfiling, DmStopProfiling
